coredata.dc:title: A novel visible light excited room temperature afterglow luminescent carbon dots/boric acid composite for information encryption and Fe3+ sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Hai-Jiao
Wang, Qian
Hu, Qi-Bin
Tian, Wei
Zhang, Peng-Xiang
Gou, Rui-Ling
Chen, Hong-Li
Wan, Guo-Yun
coredata.dc:description: Visible light excited room temperature afterglow luminescent carbon dots are rare and most of them are excited by ultraviolet light, which has potential phototoxicity, and the quantum yield of afterglow is always low. This article reports a novel room temperature afterglow luminescent carbon dots/boric acid composite (BCDs composite) that can be excited by visible light such as 450 nm LED light, flashlight, and even mobile phone flashlight with high afterglow quantum yield of 48.4 % and long afterglow lifetime of 866 ms. Based on their excellent afterglow emission property, they can be applied for information security under humidity stimulation. Under daily storage condition, the information written by BCDs composite has no afterglow, and when dried with a hair dryer, the information was lighted by afterglow. Meanwhile, the BCDs composite can also be used for highly sensitivity Fe3+ sensing with a low detection limit of 102 nM in Tris-HCl buffer. This study not only successfully developed a novel room temperature long afterglow luminescent carbon dot based nanomaterial with multiple uses and visible light excitation, but also may provide ideas for the design of subsequent room temperature afterglow carbon dots and carbon dot based sensing probes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Room temperature afterglow
Information encryption
Fe3+ sensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The synthesis of visible light excited long afterglow luminescent BCDs composite. (B) Afterglow images of the BCDs composite along with time increasing under different visible light excitation.
(A) and (B) afterglow luminescent spectra of the BCDs composite at room temperature. (C) and (D) fluorescence spectra of the BCDs composite at room temperature.
Afterglow images of patterns and numbers written on BCDs composite materials (A) Excited by 365 nm LED light. (B) Excited by 450 nm LED light. (C) Excited by Flashlight.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of BCDs composite versus various Fe3+ concentrations ranging from 0 to 50 µM. (B) Plot of (F0–Fx) / F0 versus various Fe3+ concentrations. (C) Plot of (F0–Fx) / F0 versus 400 – 2000 nM Fe3+ concentrations. (D) Plot of (F0–Fx) / F0 versus 2250 – 4750 nM Fe3+ concentrations. F0 is the PL intensity of blank BCDs composite solution and Fx is the PL intensity of BCDs composite solution with Fe3+.

head.ce:title: A novel visible light excited room temperature afterglow luminescent carbon dots/boric acid composite for information encryption and Fe3+ sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Biomedical Material, School of Life Science and Technology, Xinxiang Medical University, Xinxiang 453003, China

School of Life Science and Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Biomedical Material
School of Life Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Visible light excited room temperature afterglow luminescent carbon dots are rare and most of them are excited by ultraviolet light, which has potential phototoxicity, and the quantum yield of afterglow is always low. This article reports a novel room temperature afterglow luminescent carbon dots/boric acid composite (BCDs composite) that can be excited by visible light such as 450 nm LED light, flashlight, and even mobile phone flashlight with high afterglow quantum yield of 48.4 % and long afterglow lifetime of 866 ms. Based on their excellent afterglow emission property, they can be applied for information security under humidity stimulation. Under daily storage condition, the information written by BCDs composite has no afterglow, and when dried with a hair dryer, the information was lighted by afterglow. Meanwhile, the BCDs composite can also be used for highly sensitivity Fe3+ sensing with a low detection limit of 102 nM in Tris-HCl buffer. This study not only successfully developed a novel room temperature long afterglow luminescent carbon dot based nanomaterial with multiple uses and visible light excitation, but also may provide ideas for the design of subsequent room temperature afterglow carbon dots and carbon dot based sensing probes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Information security is of great significance in military, civil and economic fields. In the field of information security, the use of various luminous materials for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting is currently a commonly used security technology. For example, the fluorescent anti-counterfeiting encrypted information on the paper currency can easily distinguish the authenticity and prevent counterfeiting. However, fluorescence anti-counterfeiting encryption is relatively simple and easy to identify, and information security needs to be improved. Afterglow luminescent is a special form of luminescence, including thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) and room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP). Compared with fluorescence, Afterglow luminescent materials can continue to emit light after stopping excitation. This special optical property makes it widely studied and applied in the field of information security [1,2]. At present, there are three types of common afterglow luminescent materials, metal doped inorganic compounds, organometallic compounds and organic afterglow luminescent materials. For the first two categories, doping of metals (often rare earth metals) makes these afterglow luminescent materials expensive to manufacture and potentially toxic. At present, most of the organic afterglow luminescent materials are organic compounds, which are often complex to synthesize and have defects such as low inter system transition (ISC) efficiency and rapid deactivation, which greatly limits their application in the field of information security [3–5]. Therefore, the development of efficient new non-metal organic afterglow luminescent materials is an important research direction in the field of new materials and information security.

Carbon dots (CDs) is a new type of carbon-based nanomaterials developed in recent decades, which has attracted extensive attention and research enthusiasm since it was reported [6]. It is found that carbon dots has strong fluorescence emission ability, good photostability, easy surface functionalization, low toxicity, no flickering fluorescence emission, tunable emission wavelength and excellent up-conversion photoluminescence ability [7–9]. And compared with traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum, carbon dots do not contain any metal elements, therefore, thus having lower cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and environmental friendliness. These properties allow carbon dots to show a wide range of applications in various fields such as fluorescence sensing, biological imaging, energy conversion and storage [10–12]. In recent years, researchers have found that in addition to the strong fluorescence emission of carbon dots, some specially prepared carbon dots also have excellent room temperature afterglow luminescent properties. For example, Lin et al. prepared phosphorus-doped carbon dots by using phosphoric acid and ethanolamine, and achieved room temperature phosphorescence of 1.46 s [13]. Therefore, carbon dots based phosphorescent materials have broad application prospects in information security and optoelectronic devices due to their unique long-life phosphorescence characteristics and good biosecurity [14–16]. However, due to low ISC efficiency and strong non-radiative transition attenuation, the vast majority of carbon dots do not have room temperature afterglow luminescent performance, only a small number of rationally designed carbon dots showed afterglow luminescence, and the afterglow luminescent emission wavelength, afterglow luminescent lifetime and quantum yield have great room for improvement [17,18]. Therefore, the development of efficient room temperature afterglow luminescent carbon dots based materials is still an important research topic in the field of new materials.

Although after several years of development, researchers have successfully prepared a variety of room temperature afterglow luminescent carbon dots luminous materials, however, most of the reported carbon dots based afterglow luminescent materials are ultraviolet excitation, with certain phototoxicity, their emission wavelength is short and the afterglow luminescent quantum yield has a large room for improvement [15,17,19]. As we all know, compared with ultraviolet light, visible light has lower phototoxicity and higher safety, and easy to operate, suitable for a wider range of scenarios [20–22]. However, the room temperature afterglow luminescent carbon dots excited by visible light are rare [23,24]. Although limited number of visible light excited room temperature afterglow luminescent carbon dots have been reported, the phosphorescent lifetime, phosphorescent quantum yield and emission wavelength of these carbon dots still have room for improvement [20,25–28]. It is still a great challenge to develop carbon dots with long wave emission, high quantum yield and long fluorescence lifetime. In addition, some studies have found that in the preparation of room temperature afterglow materials, boric acid (BA) as a molecule matrix can provide rich hydrogen bond and B-C covalent bond sites, stabilize the triplet state, and promote afterglow luminescence [29–31]. Therefore, in this paper, we used BA as the substrate, by reacting with preprepared seed-carbon dots (seed-CDs) at high temperature, a bright yellow room temperature afterglow luminescent carbon dots/boric acid composite (BCDs composite) that can be excited by visible light was successfully prepared. The prepared BCDs composite had a high afterglow luminescent quantum yield of 48.4 % with a long lifetime of 866 ms, and they can even be excited by a mobile phone flashlight lamp. Due to the excellent room temperature afterglow luminescent properties of the prepared BCDs composite, they have been successfully used for information encryption. Meanwhile, it was also found that the prepared BCDs composite have excellent Fe3+ sensing ability in Tris-HCl buffer, which can recognize Fe3+ in the concentration range of 700–2000 nM and 2500–4500 nM with the linear correlation coefficients of 0.9916 and 0.9935, respectively, and a detection limit of 0.102 μM. These results indicated that the BCDs composite we prepared not only have excellent afterglow luminescent performance, but also had good sensing and recognition capabilities, which laid the foundation for the practical application of the carbon dots in the future.

Citric acid were obtained from innochem. All reagents were used as received without further purification. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed by the Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer system. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a JEOL JEM-F200 transmission electron microscope. FTIR spectra were measured on a FT-IR Nicolet 380 spectrometer. The fluorescence spectra and afterglow spectra of the carbon dots composite were recorded on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan). The afterglow lifetime, afterglow quantum yield and variable temperature afterglow spectra were measured by HORIBA Fluorolog-3 Modular Fluorescence Spectrometer. The fluorescence spectra of the carbon dots composite solution were recorded on a Spectrofluorometer FS5.

0.25 g 2, 7-dihydroxynaphthalene and 1.0 g citric acid were weighed into 12 mL deionized water and ultrasonic for an appropriate time. Then After hydrothermal treatment of the above solution at 180 °C for 6 h, the solution was filtered by a 450 nm film filter to remove large particles and then freeze-dried in a vacuum freeze dryer. In order to further remove impurities, the resulting freeze-dried product is then re-dissolved in deionized water and filtered with a 450 nm filter membrane again, and then freeze-dried to obtain fluorescent seed-carbon dots. The synthesis of carbon dots/BA composite (BCDs composite) is similar to the previously reported method [32,33]. Briefly, 400 µL 40 mg/mL seed carbon dots, 1 g boric acid and 35 mL deionized water were added to the beaker and ultrasonic for an appropriate time, and then the beaker was covered with a perforated tin foil and then reacted at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling and grinding, the BCDs composite is obtained.

The BCDs composite is dispersed in an aqueous solution, which is then used to write various numbers and patterns. After drying the written numbers and patterns, use different excitation sources respectively and take pictures with mobile phones.

BCDs composite (0.1 mg mL−1) Tric-HCl solution was used as the blank solution and then different concentrations of Fe3+ were added to the above BCDs composite solution, and the mixed solutions were equilibrated for 8 min. In addition, the fluorescence emission spectra were collected at an excitation wavelength of 405 nm on a Spectrofluorometer FS5. The selectivity for Fe3+ was measured by adding other metal ion instead of Fe3+ in the similar way.

The schematic diagram of carbon dots synthesis is shown in Fig. 1 . Firstly, citric acid and 2,7-dihydroxynaphthalene were used for hydrothermal reaction to obtain seed-carbon dots (seed-CDs). Then, the seed-CDs was heated and reacted with BA at 180 °C for 5 h according to the method reported in previous literature [32]. After cooling, the solid carbon dots/BA composite obtained from the reaction was ground to obtain powdery carbon dots/BA composite (Named as BCDs composite). After the 450 nm LED light is turned off, it can see that the BCDs composite could emit bright yellow afterglow luminescence, and the afterglow can last for several seconds (Fig. 1B and Video S1). Similarly, using a white light flashlight as the excitation source, the BCDs composite was also able to emit bright yellow afterglow luminescence that lasted for about 11 s after the light was turned off (Fig. 1B and Video S2). Even when illuminated with a mobile phone flashlight, bright yellow afterglow luminescence could still be observed when the light source was turned off (Fig. 1B and Video S3). These results indicated that the prepared BCDs composite have successfully achieved high efficiency visible light excited and long wavelength yellow room temperature afterglow luminescent emission. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig. S1) showed that the carbon dots were quasi spherical with the average particle size of ∼ 2.8 nm and a well-resolved lattice spacing of 0.21 and 0.32 nm, corresponding to the (100) and (002) lattice [34,35]. The crystalline structure of BCDs composite were also investigated by the X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum. As shown in Fig. S2, a strong broad diffraction peak at 2θ = ∼ 23° and a weak shoulder diffraction peak at 2θ = ∼ 43° were detected, corresponding to the [002] and [100] lattice planes of nanographite, which was consistent with the results in high-resolution TEM image [36–38]. In addition, the XRD spectrum also showed a weak and sharp diffraction peak at 2θ = ∼ 28°, indicating the presence of a small amount of B2O3 in the BCDs composite [39].

The composition of BCDs composite were further in investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). As shown in Fig. S3A-D, the full spectrum of XPS shows that BCDs composite mainly exhibited three peaks at 194 eV, 285 eV and 533 eV, respectively, corresponding to B1s, C1s and O1s groups [40]. The high-resolution C1s spectrum of BCDs composite exhibited three peaks at 284.9 eV, 286.5 eV and 288.9 eV, respectively, corresponding to C-C/C = C, C-O and C = O groups [41,42]. The B1s spectrum of BCDs composite displayed two bands centered at 193.4 eV and 194.5 eV, attributed to B2O3 and B-O groups, respectively [33,43]. The high-resolution O1s spectrum can be fitted with two peaks at 533.0 eV and 534.0 eV, attributed to C–O–C/C–O–B and O–C = O/C–OH groups, respectively [44–46]. These results were also confirmed by FTIR spectrum, and as shown in Fig. S3E, The absorbance characteristic peak at ∼ 3223 cm−1 and ∼ 1700 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of –OH and C = O, respectively. The strong absorption bands at ∼ 1446 cm−1 and ∼ 1193 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of B − O, respectively [20,47,48]. These results indicate that the dehydration condensation reaction took place between boric acid itself and the seed-CDs at high temperature, and B-O-B and B-O-C chemical bonds may be formed. The existence of these chemical bonds makes the carbon dots better confined in the boric acid matrix, thus obtaining room temperature afterglow luminescence.

Then UV–Vis absorption spectrum were first used to investigate the optical properties of BCDs composite (Fig. S4). The BCDs composite showed multiple absorption peaks at 245 nm, 303 nm, and 415 nm. The absorption peaks at 245 nm and 303 nm are attributed to the π-π* transition, while the wide absorption peaks at 415 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition [49,50]. Subsequently, the afterglow luminescent spectra and fluorescence spectra of the BCDs composite were studied. As shown in Fig. 2 A and B, the BCDs composite could emit room temperature afterglow luminescence either by 365 excitation or by visible light above 400 nm. The afterglow emission intensity reached the maximum under the excitation wavelength of 445 and 450 nm. At the optimum excitation wavelength of 450 nm, there are two emission peaks in the afterglow spectrum of the BCDs composite, which are located at 513 nm and 578 nm respectively, and the intensity of the emission peak at 578 nm is much higher than that at 513 nm. Based on the BCDs composite afterglow spectrum at 450 nm excitation, the commission Internationale de L’Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of the phosphorescence spectra was calculated to be (0.46, 0.48), corresponding to the yellow afterglow of these BCDs composite (Fig. S5). Similar to the phosphorescence spectra, these BCDs composite also showed two fluorescence emission peaks under different excitation wavelengths, and their peak positions are almost the same as those in the phosphorescence spectra under corresponding excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2C and D).

In order to verify whether the afterglow of BCDs composite belongs to phosphorescence or TADF, the afterglow spectra at different temperature was measured. As shown in Fig. S6A, with the increase of temperature, the intensity of the peak in the low energy region of the afterglow spectrum gradually decreased, while the peak in the high energy region did not shows a peak below 220 K, and while showed a weak intensity peak at 298 K. This indicates that the peak in the lower energy region should be attributed to phosphorescence, while the peak in the higher energy region should be attributed to TADF [51]. Meanwhile, we also measured the phosphorescence lifetime of the BCDs composite at different temperatures at 578 nm. As shown in Fig. S6B, at 77 k, the phosphorescence lifetime reached 1102 ms. As the temperature increased, the phosphorescence lifetime gradually decreased, which once again confirms that the emission peak located in the low energy region in the afterglow spectrum should belong to phosphorescence rather than TADF [52]. When the temperature reaches room temperature (298 k), its phosphorescence lifetime was 866 ms, which is comparable to previous literature reports [20,28,35,53]. Furthermore, the afterglow quantum yield of the BCDs composite at 450 nm was also measured, and the results showed that the BCDs composite had a high afterglow quantum yield of up to 48.4 % (Fig. S7), which is higher than many literature reports [2].

BA was a very common substrate for the preparation of room temperature afterglow luminescent carbon dots based materials and some researchers have found that boric acid as a matrix embedded with carbon dots can be used to obtain efficient afterglow luminescent materials through multiple actions. After the reaction between boric acid and carbon dots, hydrogen bonding, B2O3 polycrystalline confinement, B-C covalence, and glass state confinement effect of boric acid may be existed [33,54,55]. These effects cooperate with each other to greatly inhibit the vibration of the luminescent center of the carbon dots, thus stabilizing the triplet state of the luminescent center and reducing its non-radiative transition, and obtaining long afterglow luminescence at room temperature. In addition, it also can be seen from the results of the fluorescence spectra of these BCDs composite that the peak position of the fluorescence spectrum is almost coincides with its afterglow spectrum (Fig. 2), which indicates that the energy level difference (Δ E ST) between T1 and S1 is very small, which will be very conducive to promote effective ISC process from singlet to triplet excited states and reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) process from the T1 to S1 state, and ultimately produce efficient room temperature persistence emission.

Based on the excellent afterglow emission property of the BCDs composite, it can be predicted that these BCDs composite have good application potential in information encryption. As shown in Fig. 3 , the BCDs composite solution were used to write different patterns and numbers on the filter paper, and after drying, different excitation sources (365 nm LED lamp, 450 nm LED lamp and flashlight) to excite them respectively. After turning off the excitation light, it can clearly see that the patterns and numbers written by BCDs composite solution are lighted by the afterglow. In addition, the blue fluorescent dye was also used as interference for information anti-counterfeiting. Under the 365 nm lamp, the number '222′ is clearly lighted, and when the 365 nm lamp is turned off, the number '2′ in the middle cannot be seen because it does not contain BCDs composite, while the other two '2′ are clearly seen. In general, afterglow luminescent nanomaterials often have no afterglow emission in water due to hydrogen bond destruction and the presence of dissolved oxygen. Therefore, the patterns and numbers written by BCDs composite solution were put in an environment with high humidity (40 %) for 2 days, and then performed afterglow imaging. It can be seen that the patterns and numbers din not emit afterglow after absorbing water, which means that the information written by the carbon dots is well hidden. Afterwards, the numbers and patterns were dried with a hair dryer again, and then carried out afterglow imaging. The results showed that the numbers and patterns were lighted up again after drying. In other words, the information written with carbon dots can be dynamically hidden and revealed by controlling the water absorption and drying process, which also shows that the BCDs composite we prepare can achieve high-level confidentiality of information.

From the FTIR and XPS results, it can be seen that the BCDs composite contain –OH and B-O groups, which have good bonding effect with some metal ions. Therefore, the BCDs composite were also used for sensing Fe3+ ions. To prevent metal ion hydrolysis, Tris-HCl with pH 2 was used as the working solution. As can be seen from Fig. S8, the optimal excitation wavelength of BCDs composite in Tris-HCl solution was 405 nm, and the maximum emission peak was located at 530 nm. After adding Fe3+ ions, the fluorescence of the BCDs composite solution was quenched, and after 8 min of reaction, the fluorescence intensity remained almost unchanged, indicating that the binding of carbon dots and Fe3+ ions took about 8 min (Fig. S9). The fluorescence titration curve shows that the fluorescence intensity of BCDs composite decreases gradually with the continuous addition of Fe3+ (Fig. 4 A). Fig. 4B−D displays the plot of (F0–Fx) / F0 ratio versus Fe3+ concentration, where F0 is the PL intensity of blank BCDs composite solution and Fx is the PL intensity of BCDs composite solution with Fe3+. It was revealed that between 400–2000 nM, and 2250–4750 nM, the fluorescence of BCDs composite presents a good linear relationship with Fe3+ concentration with the linear correlation coefficients are 0.9916 and 0.9935, respectively. Furthermore, according to previous report, LOD = (3.3 σ/k) is used to calculate the limit of detection (LOD), where σ is the standard deviation of intercept and k is the slope of the linear calibration plot [56]. Based on these above results, the calculated LOD is 0.102 μM, indicating that the BCDs composite can be used as a highly sensitive sensing probe for Fe3+ ions in Tris-HCl buffer. To further explore the selectivity of BCDs composite as a sensor for Fe3+, the (F0–Fx) / F0 ratio was also evaluated after adding different metal ions. As shown in Fig. S10, except for Fe3+ ions, other ions have little influence on the fluorescence intensity of BCDs composite, and only the addition of Fe3+ can significantly reduce the fluorescence intensity of BCDs composite. Meanwhile, it can also be seen more intuitively from the photos that the fluorescence of the BCDs composite is still very bright after adding other ions, and the fluorescence is almost completely quenched after adding Fe3+ (Fig. S11). These resluts suggest that Fe3+ has a stronger affinity with BCDs composite than other metal ions and BCDs composite can be used as a high sensitive and selectivity probe for the detection of Fe3+ in Tris-HCl buffer.

In order to explore the quenching mechanism between BCDs composite and Fe3+ ions, the fluorescence lifetimes of BCDs composite in the presence and absence of Fe3+ were measured. As shown in Fig. S12, after adding 2 and 10 uM Fe3+, the fluorescence lifetime of BCDs composite decreased slightly from 8.5 ns to 8.36 ns and 8.34 ns, respectively. After the addition of 50 uM Fe3+, the fluorescence lifetime of BCDs composite is greatly reduced to 7.87 ns. According to previous literature reports, at lower concentration of Fe3+ ions, the fluorescence quenching mechanism may be static, while at higher concentration of Fe3+ ions, the quenching mechanism can be dynamic[57,58]. Under static quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime should remain almost unchanged, while under dynamic quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime will decrease. According to our fluorescence data, the quenching mechanism of Fe3+ should contain both static and dynamic mechanisms: that is, under low concentration of Fe3+, the static quenching mechanism is dominant, and the fluorescence lifetime is slightly reduced. At high concentrations of Fe3+, the dynamic quenching mechanism is dominant, and the fluorescence lifetime is greatly reduced. In order to further explore the practical applicability of BCDs composite for Fe3+ detection, we used the standard addition recovery method to detect Fe3+ concentration in environmental water samples. As shown in Table S1, 30, 40 and 50 uM Fe3+ river water samples were prepared respectively, and then 100 uL of the river water sample was added to the Tris-Hcl buffer (2900 μL) to measure the fluorescence of BCDs composite. Then the concentration and recovery rate of Fe3+ ions in river water were calculated according to the standard curve and dilution ratio. It can be seen from the results that the recovery rate of the added sample is no more than 107 %, which has a good recovery and accuracy, indicating that the BCDs composite has a broad application prospect for Fe3+ detection in the actual water.

In conclusion, a novel visible light excited long afterglow luminescent carbon dots based material have successfully prepared by using boric acid as a substrate and reacted with seed carbon dots at high temperatures. The obtained BCDs composite can emit a bright yellow afterglow under the excitation of visible light, such as 450 nm LED lamps, flashlight, and even mobile phone flashlight. To our excitement, the afterglow quantum yield is as high as 48.4 % and the afterglow lifetime is 866 ms, which is much higher than many previous literature reports. Based on their excellent afterglow luminescence performance, they have been successfully applied to information encryption. The numbers and patterns written by BCDs composite aqueous solution can be excited by visible light like a powder and emit a bright afterglow after drying. Moreover, after these numbers and patterns are placed in a humid environment for a period of time, their afterglow is quenched due to water absorption, and the written information is well hidden. When dried again, the figures and patterns are lighted again by the afterglow, demonstrating a high level of information protection potential of BCDs composite. In addition, due to these BCDs composite contain many functional groups such as –OH and B-O, which are easy to bind to metal ions, the prepared BCDs composite also been successfully used for high sensitivity and selective sensing of Fe3+ ions with the linear range between 400–2000 nM and 2250–4850 nM in Tris-HCl buffer. The linear coefficient are 0.9916 and 0.9935, respectively and the detection limit is 0.102 uM. The detection of Fe3+ in actual water samples also proves that the BCDs composite have good practical application potential. In summary, these results not only demonstrate that the visible light excited room temperature long afterglow luminescent carbon dots we prepared have various practical applications, but may also provide ideas for the future development of room temperature long afterglow luminescent carbon dots and cation sensing carbon dots based probes.

Hai-Jiao Wang: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Qian Wang: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Qi-Bin Hu: Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wei Tian: Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Peng-Xiang Zhang: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Rui-Ling Gou: Data curation, Conceptualization. Hong-Li Chen: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Guo-Yun Wan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support from the Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Xinxiang Medical University (XYBSKYZZ201716), the Key Technologies R&D Program of Henan Province (232102310377 and 242102521008), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (242300421534), the Open Project Program of the Third Affiliated Hospital of Xinxiang Medical University (No. KFKTZD202102) and the College Students' Innovative Entrepreneurial Training Plan Program (202310472046).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110580.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent Progress in Inorganic Afterglow Materials: Mechanisms, Persistent Luminescent Properties, Modulating Methods, and Bioimaging Applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Yang, S. Gai, H. Ding, D. Yang, L. Feng, P. Yang, Recent Progress in Inorganic Afterglow Materials: Mechanisms, Persistent Luminescent Properties, Modulating Methods, and Bioimaging Applications, Adv. Opt. Mater. (2023) 2202382, https://doi.org/10.1002/adom.202202382.

Small
